summary: Jeff Atwood argues that requiring users to log in before experiencing a website's value is a critical mistake that drives away the vast majority of potential users. He uses Facebook's API documentation as an example of this anti-pattern, noting that login barriers are not mere friction but 'brick walls' that stop users cold. The post highlights alternative approaches like Geni's streamlined onboarding and the anonymous cookie-based accounts used by Netvibes and Pageflakes. These services let users experience full functionality before asking them to commit to an account, allowing relationships to develop naturally over time.
key_points: Login barriers cause 99% of users to simply leave rather than sign up, making them far worse than ordinary UI friction
Users cannot evaluate whether a site is worth joining without first seeing what's behind the login wall
Anonymous cookie-based accounts allow users to experience full site functionality before committing to registration
Smart sites let the relationship develop naturally, waiting until users have genuine motivation to create an account
Using email as user ID eliminates the extra friction of choosing a username during signup
money_quotes: When presented with the login barrier, I did what 99% of all the people who encounter a login barrier do: I turned back.
Login barriers are far worse than friction – they're a brick wall.
Without sneaking behind the barrier, if only for a moment, how can the user possibly know if your site is worth the hassle of signing up?
You can do much, much better than the abysmal login barrier status quo.
The deep principle at work is that a site doesn't need to rush to secure a relationship with a visitor.
By removing the login barrier and blurring the line between anonymous users and named users, you're likely to gain a lot more of the latter.
key_insight: The best way to convert anonymous visitors into registered users is to let them experience your product's full value first, allowing the relationship to develop naturally rather than demanding commitment upfront.
